6: The seven-nation apostasy list in v6 is the most comprehensive enumeration of foreign gods in Judges, spanning the entire political geography of Israel's neighbors: Baals (Canaanite storm deity), Ashtaroth (Canaanite goddess of fertility and war), gods of Aram (Syrian pantheon, primarily Hadad/Ben-Hadad), gods of Sidon (Phoenician pantheon, primarily Baal and Asherah), gods of Moab (primarily Chemosh — the Mesha Stele confirms Chemosh as Moab's national deity), gods of Ammon (primarily Milcom/Molech), gods of the Philistines (primarily Dagon — confirmed by the Ugaritic texts and the later Temple of Dagon at Ashdod in 1 Sam 5). The geographic span — Syria to the north, Phoenicia to the northwest, Moab and Ammon to the east, Philistia to the west — means Israel has worshiped the gods of every surrounding people simultaneously. This total syncretism is the culmination of the progressive apostasy the book has been documenting.
11: YHWH's covenant-lawsuit speech in vv11-14 is the most confrontational divine address in Judges. The speech form is the rîḇ (covenant lawsuit) — YHWH speaks as the wronged covenant-lord to the unfaithful vassal. The historical recital in v11-12 is evidence: 'Did I not save you from the Egyptians and from the Amorites, from the sons of Ammon and from the Philistines? The Sidonians also, and Amalek and Maon oppressed you, and you cried out to me, and I saved you from their hand.' The recital of past deliverance makes the current apostasy inexcusable — Israel has received covenant benefits and then abandoned the covenant party who provided them. This is the same pattern as the prophets' lawsuits: 'What more could I have done for my vineyard?' (Isaiah 5:4).
14: YHWH's sarcastic dismissal — 'Go and cry out to the gods whom you have chosen; let them save you in the time of your distress' — is unprecedented in Judges. Prior cycles moved directly from Israel's cry to YHWH's compassion and action (Judges 2:18 — 'When YHWH raised up judges for them, YHWH was with the judge, and he saved them from the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge, for YHWH was moved to pity by their groaning'). Here the compassion is withheld and replaced by divine sarcasm. This represents a theological development within the book's cycle: YHWH is not a vending machine for salvation but a covenant person whose patience has been exhausted. The episode anticipates the NT teaching that it is possible to 'grieve the Holy Spirit' (Ephesians 4:30) — divine patience is genuine, not infinite.
16: 'And they put away the foreign gods from among them and served YHWH, and he became impatient over the misery of Israel.' The divine pathos expressed here — YHWH's soul being 'shortened/distressed' (wattiqqṣar napšô) over Israel's ʿāmal (misery/toil) — is one of the OT's most striking expressions of divine inner life. The verb qāṣar with nepeš means to feel constrained, impatient, grieved — the same idiom appears in Numbers 21:4 where Israel's 'soul was impatient' on the wilderness journey. YHWH's inner emotional response to Israel's suffering is emphasized across the prophets (Isaiah 63:9 — 'In all their affliction he was afflicted'; Jeremiah 31:20 — 'my heart yearns for him; I will surely have mercy on him'). The ANE comparative literature on divine emotion (the Mesopotamian Laments describe the gods' anguish over destroyed cities) shows that expressing divine pathos was a recognized theological category — but Israel's tradition uniquely roots it in covenant relationship rather than impersonal fate.
1: Tola son of Puah, son of Dodo, from the tribe of Issachar — one of the 'minor judges' about whom minimal narrative information is preserved. The minor judges (Tola, Jair, Ibzan, Elon, Abdon; cf. Judges 12:8-15) appear in lists that record their judgeships, tribal origin, and deaths without deliverance narratives. Scholars debate whether these were local civic leaders/arbiters (legal judges) or whether they also delivered Israel from enemies, with the tradition having lost their narratives. Tola lived at Shamir in the hill country of Ephraim — the same general territory as Deborah (Judges 4:5) and the house of Joseph.
2: Tola judged Israel twenty-three years and died and was buried in Shamir. The twenty-three year tenure is one of the longer minor-judge periods. The minor judges' records preserve the names of their burial sites — Shamir, Kamon (v5) — suggesting these details were preserved in local tradition or administrative records. The combination of tribal affiliation, parentage, location, tenure length, and burial site suggests a formal archival tradition behind the minor-judge lists, possibly associated with the sanctuary at Shiloh or local tribal administrative records.
3: Jair the Gileadite arose after Tola and judged Israel twenty-two years. The double qualification 'the Gileadite' identifies him with the Transjordanian tribal territory east of the Jordan — Gilead was a major region including parts of Gad and Manasseh east of the Jordan. The Transjordanian judges (Jair; later Jephthah, also from Gilead) reflect the full geographic scope of the judge tradition, which was not limited to the west-bank tribes. Jair's name is also that of an earlier Manassite clan-leader (Numbers 32:41; Deuteronomy 3:14) who had settled east-Manasseh territory — the judge may be associated with this clan tradition.
4: Jair had thirty sons who rode on thirty donkeys and had thirty cities — 'Havvoth-jair' ('tent-villages of Jair') — in the land of Gilead. The thirty-son, thirty-donkey, thirty-city correlation is a standard chieftain's formula indicating both political influence (many sons = multiple heirs and allies) and economic control (each city = a revenue center). Donkeys as mounts for notables recurs throughout Judges (the white donkeys of the Song of Deborah, Judges 5:10) and reflects the pre-horse transportation of the Iron Age I period. The Havvoth-jair are mentioned in Numbers 32:41 and Deuteronomy 3:14, suggesting long-standing clan tenure in this territory.
5: Jair died and was buried in Kamon. Kamon's location is disputed — Jerome identified it with a site in the Decapolis region; modern candidates include Tell el-Qaimun or a site in northern Gilead. The terse burial notice closes the Jair entry with the same archival finality as the other minor-judge records. What is notable about Jair's entry as a whole is what it lacks: no enemy oppression, no cry to YHWH, no Spirit-empowerment, no deliverance narrative — only the quiet civic tenure of a leader whose era was apparently one of peace and stability, perhaps because the covenant remained intact during his administration.
7: 'And the anger of YHWH was kindled against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of the Philistines and into the hand of the Ammonites.' The double oppression — Philistines (coastal plain, west) and Ammonites (Transjordan, east) — is unique in Judges and creates a geographically comprehensive squeeze on Israel from both sides of the Jordan. This double threat sets the stage for two separate deliverers: Jephthah against Ammon (Judges 11-12) and eventually Samson against the Philistines (Judges 13-16). The narrative structure of Judges 10-16 is thus a double-strand that the editor has interleaved: the Ammonite crisis is resolved first, the Philistine crisis extends through the Samson cycle.
8: 'They crushed and oppressed the people of Israel that year. For eighteen years they oppressed all the people of Israel who were beyond the Jordan in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gilead.' The eighteen-year oppression — concentrated in the Transjordanian Gilead region — explains why a Gileadite leader (Jephthah) will emerge as the deliverer. The Ammonites claiming Gilead as their historical territory (Judges 11:13) is the political claim that underlies the military pressure; the oppression is the enforcement arm of a territorial dispute that will require legal-diplomatic as well as military resolution. Jephthah's double role as negotiator and warrior corresponds to this dual (diplomatic + military) character of the crisis.
9: 'The Ammonites also crossed the Jordan to fight against Judah and against Benjamin and against the house of Ephraim, so that Israel was severely distressed.' The Ammonite crossing of the Jordan from Transjordan into the west-bank tribal territories elevated the crisis from a Transjordanian border dispute to an existential threat to the entire tribal confederation. The specific naming of Judah, Benjamin, and Ephraim — the southern, central, and central-northern tribes — suggests a broad Ammonite offensive rather than a targeted raid. The phrase 'severely distressed' (wayyēṣar lĕyiśrāʾēl mĕʾōd) is the standard pre-deliverance vocabulary, signaling the imminent cry and divine response.
10: 'And the people of Israel cried out to YHWH, saying, We have sinned against you, because we have forsaken our God and have served the Baals.' The covenant confession includes specific acknowledgment: 'we have forsaken our God and served the Baals.' The specificity of the confession — naming the precise violation (Baal worship) rather than offering a generic apology — is notable. This is one of the more substantive confessions in Judges, combined with the subsequent YHWH-response in v11-14 that demands more than verbal confession. The exchange in vv10-16 is theologically the most developed divine-Israel dialogue in the book.
12: YHWH's expanded recital of past deliverances — Egypt, Amorites, Ammon, Philistines, Sidon, Amalek, Maon — is the most comprehensive historical review in Judges, demonstrating through accumulation that Israel has been rescued from all these same or similar threats before. The inclusion of Maon (possibly the Maonites, cf. 2 Chronicles 26:7) among the deliverances is difficult; some manuscripts read 'Midian.' The rhetorical point is a list of precedents: YHWH has proven his willingness to rescue Israel repeatedly, which makes the continued apostasy all the more inexcusable — they sin against a demonstrated track record of covenant faithfulness.
13: 'Yet you have forsaken me and served other gods; therefore I will save you no more.' YHWH's declaration of withdrawal is unprecedented in Judges — no previous judge cycle features YHWH threatening to end the deliverer pattern entirely. The threat 'I will save you no more' is a conditional covenant curse (cf. Deuteronomy 28:25: 'YHWH will cause you to be defeated before your enemies') taken to its logical extreme. The purpose of this unprecedented threat in the narrative is to force a more fundamental response from Israel than the rote cry-repent-receive pattern that the earlier cycles had established. YHWH refuses to be a predictable rescue machine.
15: 'And the people of Israel said to YHWH, We have sinned; do to us whatever seems good to you. Only please deliver us this day.' The second confession (after v10) adds the submission formula: 'do to us whatever seems good to you' — an unconditional surrender to YHWH's judgment, followed by the plea for deliverance. The combination of total submission and continued petition is the proper covenant posture that YHWH's v13-14 address demanded. This confession moves from formula (v10) to genuine surrender — the difference between apologizing for consequences and accepting the covenant lord's authority over the situation entirely. It is met by the divine relenting of v16.
17: 'Then the Ammonites were called to arms, and they encamped in Gilead. And the people of Israel came together, and they encamped at Mizpah.' The military mobilization on both sides — Ammon in Gilead, Israel at Mizpah — sets up the leadership crisis of v18. Mizpah in Gilead (different from the west-bank Mizpah of Judges 20-21 and Samuel) was an important Transjordanian sanctuary; its name means 'watchtower.' The assembly at a sacred site before battle is standard Israelite holy-war procedure; the crisis is that there is no designated military leader, which is why the question of v18 becomes urgent.
18: 'And the people, the leaders of Gilead, said one to another, Who is the man who will begin to fight against the Ammonites? He shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead.' The Gileadite leaders' question — and their offer of permanent leadership to whoever answers it — is the setup for Jephthah's call in Judges 11. The offer of rōʾš (head/chief) status creates a crisis of legitimacy similar to the one Abimelech exploited in Judges 9: when leadership is offered to whoever will fight, rather than given by YHWH to whom he chooses, the result is predictably problematic. Jephthah's negotiation of his leadership terms (Judges 11:9-11) shows he recognized the offer's structural parallels to Abimelech's coup and insisted on covenant-proper authorization.
